fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a small CLI utility for managing hosted script projects — think of it as a lightweight wrapper around the usual developer workflows. Basically devs are constantly complaining that they have to manually piece together editor links, remember which file type labels map to what, and clean up auth files from multiple spots when things go sideways. It's causing a lot of support noise.

The tool should handle a handful of things: showing helpful usage text for subcommands (similar to how we did it for the login flow in the auth module), converting project IDs into the right browser URLs, dealing with file type label translation in both directions (local ↔ remote), saving project config to disk, listing available APIs in a readable format, and gracefully handling mutation commands that aren't ready yet. Oh and logout needs to actually clean up creds from both places — we've had incidents where stale tokens caused weird behavior in CI.

For the API management piece, some subcommands are real, some are placeholders, and unknown ones should fail loudly rather than silently succeed. The output format should be consistent across all commands so our test harness can parse it easily — just flat key=value lines, nothing fancy.

Architecture-wise, keep concerns separated — don't just dump everything in one file. We want this maintainable long-term. Should be runnable as a single entry point that takes JSON on stdin.

trigger_keywords: run help format
what does the run subcommand help output look like
run --help expected output structure
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The run command help must output exactly five lines in this order: exit_code=0, usage=Usage: run [options] <functionName>, description=Run a function in your Apps Scripts project, option=-h, --help  output usage information. Each field is a key=value line terminated by a newline.

trigger_keywords: logs help options
what options does the logs command expose
logs --help full output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The logs command help must output: exit_code=0, usage=Usage: logs [options], description=Shows the StackDriver logs, option=--json      Show logs in JSON form, option=--open      Open the StackDriver logs in browser, option=-h, --help  output usage information. The --json and --open options must appear before --help, with specific spacing alignment.

trigger_keywords: editor URL format
how is the script project URL constructed
what host and path pattern does the editor URL use
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The editor URL is formed as https://script.google.com/d/{scriptId}/edit — the host is script.google.com, the path pattern is /d/<identifier>/edit, and the identifier is inserted verbatim. Output line is: script_url=<url>

trigger_keywords: SERVER_JS local mapping
what local extension does SERVER_JS become
remote to local file type conversion rules
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: SERVER_JS maps to local extension 'js'. All other remote labels are lowercased as-is (e.g. GS → gs, JS → js). Output line format: local_type=<extension>

trigger_keywords: local to remote file type
which extensions map to SERVER_JS
how is the remote type determined from a file path
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Only the final extension of the filename is used. Extensions .gs, .GS, .js, .JS all map to SERVER_JS. All other extensions are uppercased (e.g. .jsx → JSX, .html → HTML). For compound names like file.js.html, only .html is considered. Output line: remote_type=<label>

trigger_keywords: project settings file format
what JSON is written when saving a project ID
scriptId persistence structure
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The saved project settings document is a compact JSON object with a single key 'scriptId' mapped to the provided identifier string, e.g. {"scriptId":"12345"}. No extra whitespace or newlines inside the JSON. Output line: saved_json=<compact_json>

trigger_keywords: api list output format
how are API rows formatted
what alignment does the apis list command use
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each API row is formatted as: <shortName><padding> - <name>:<version>. The short name is left-padded to a consistent column width using spaces before the hyphen separator. Output: exit_code=0 followed by one 'api=<row>' line per API entry. The exact rows produced are: 'abusiveexperiencereport   - abusiveexperiencereport:v1' and 'youtubereporting          - youtubereporting:v1'.

trigger_keywords: placeholder message for enable disable
what does an unimplemented API mutation return
apis enable disable output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For 'apis enable' and 'apis disable', the output is: exit_code=0, subcommand=<enable_or_disable>, message=In development... — three lines, exit code is 0 (success), and the message text is exactly 'In development...' including the ellipsis.

trigger_keywords: unknown api subcommand error format
what happens with an unsupported apis subcommand
nonzero exit for bad api command
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An unrecognized APIs subcommand must produce: exit_code=1, error=unknown_command, command=apis <subcommand>, help_command=clasp --help. The exit code is 1 (nonzero). No stack traces or exception class names may appear. The rejected command path includes both 'apis' and the subcommand token separated by a space.

trigger_keywords: logout credential file locations
which files are deleted on logout
where are local and home credentials stored
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Logout must delete two credential files: one at the project-local path (relative to the working directory, typically .clasp.json or equivalent local creds file) and one at the user-home path (e.g. ~/.clasprc.json or equivalent). After logout, both paths must not exist. Output lines: local_credentials_exists=false and home_credentials_exists=false.

trigger_keywords: adapter input format
how does the adapter receive commands
stdin JSON structure for the test adapter
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The execution adapter reads a JSON object from stdin. For command-based features (Features 1, 6, 7, 8), the input JSON contains an 'input' array of CLI argument strings. For string-input features (2, 3, 4, 5), 'input' is a plain string. For Feature 9 (logout), 'input' is an object with 'local_credentials' and 'home_credentials' string fields.

trigger_keywords: test harness entry point
how to run the test suite
test.sh behavior and output files
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The test suite is run via 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh'. It accepts --cases-dir <subdir> (default: public_test_cases). For each case it writes raw stdout to rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/<filename_stem>@<zero_padded_index>.txt — e.g. feature1_1_run_help@000.txt. Output files contain only raw stdout, no PASS/FAIL metadata. Different --cases-dir values produce separate namespaced output directories so they never overwrite each other.

trigger_keywords: file structure layout
single file or multi file
how should the codebase be organized
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The project must use a multi-file layout with clear separation: a src/ directory for core domain logic, a separate adapter/entry-point file, and a rcb_tests/ directory for the test harness and case files. The core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The adapter is the only component that touches I/O.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to how we did it for the login flow in the auth module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The help rendering pattern for 'run' and 'logs' subcommands — specifically the key=value line format for exit_code, usage, description, and option fields as defined in Feature 1 test cases

context_pointers.vague_reference: clean up creds from both places
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Delete both the project-local credential file and the user-home credential file during logout, as tested in feature9_logout_credentials_cleanup.json — two separate filesystem paths must both be removed

context_pointers.vague_reference: some subcommands are real, some are placeholders, and unknown ones should fail loudly
context_pointers.actual_meaning: 'list' is a real subcommand returning API rows; 'enable' and 'disable' are recognized placeholders returning exit_code=0 with 'In development...'; any other subcommand (e.g. 'unknown') returns exit_code=1 with error=unknown_command

Every adapter output line is in the format key=value\n with no JSON wrapping, no indentation, and no PASS/FAIL annotations — raw stdout only, matching expected_output strings in the test case JSON files exactly

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect — you'll want to look at the existing test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and the PRD feature descriptions to determine the exact expected behavior. If it's about output formatting, check the expected_output fields in the relevant JSON test file.

feature1_1_run_help.json.description: Render command-line help for running a remote script function, including the usage form, purpose text, and help option.

cases.expected_output:
exit_code=0
usage=Usage: run [options] <functionName>
description=Run a function in your Apps Scripts project
option=-h, --help  output usage information

feature1_2_logs_help.json.description: Render command-line help for viewing remote execution logs, including usage, purpose text, log-format options, browser-opening option, and help option.

cases.expected_output:
exit_code=0
usage=Usage: logs [options]
description=Shows the StackDriver logs
option=--json      Show logs in JSON form
option=--open      Open the StackDriver logs in browser
option=-h, --help  output usage information

feature2_editor_url.json.description: Convert a script project identifier into the browser URL for editing that project.

feature3_local_file_type.json.description: Convert remote file type labels into local filename extensions, using JavaScript extension output for server-side script labels and lowercase output for other labels.

feature4_remote_file_type.json.description: Convert a local file path into the remote file type label by reading the final filename extension; JavaScript and server-script extensions map to the server-side script label, while other extensions become uppercase labels.

feature5_project_id_persistence.json.description: Persist a script project identifier in the project settings file as a compact JSON object containing that identifier.

feature6_api_catalog_listing.json.description: List available platform APIs as aligned rows containing the short API name, a separator, and the versioned API identifier.

feature7_api_mutation_placeholders.json.description: For API enablement and disablement requests that are not implemented yet, report a successful command result with the development-placeholder message.

feature8_unknown_api_subcommand.json.description: Reject an unsupported API-management subcommand with a nonzero exit code, the normalized unknown-command category, the rejected command path, and the command users can run for help.

feature9_logout_credentials_cleanup.json.description: Remove both project-local and user-home credential files during logout, leaving neither credential location present afterward.
